question: [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged signature submitter, under the precondition that authorization.authorizer has NOT yet called midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), true, authorizer), trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized(auth, sig) with a valid EIP-712 signature, causing the IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).setIsAuthorized call at line 47 to revert with Unauthorized() while the nonce at line 26 has already been incremented in the same transaction, violating the invariant that a failed authorization attempt must not advance the authorizer's nonce, causing scoped impact: user's nonce is burned on a revert path, permanently invalidating any pre-signed authorization at that nonce? Proof idea: deploy EcrecoverAuthorizer without pre-authorizing it in Midnight, sign a valid Authorization struct, call setIsAuthorized, assert that nonce[authorizer] is unchanged after revert and that the pre-signed message at nonce 0 is still usable.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.isAuthorized] Can an unprivileged Midnight-authorized operator (isAuthorized[victim][operator] == true), under the precondition that EcrecoverAuthorizer is also authorized in Midnight by the victim, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with an Authorization struct where authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, signed by operator (accepted via the IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(authorization.authorizer, signer) branch at line 34), causing midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim) to execute, violating the invariant that only the authorizer's own key or an explicitly EcrecoverAuthorizer-scoped signer should be able to grant new authorizations via the periphery, causing scoped impact: operator's Midnight authorization implicitly grants them the power to further delegate full Midnight control to arbitrary third parties via EcrecoverAuthorizer? Proof idea: unit test where operator is authorized in Midnight by victim, operator signs an Authorization granting attacker full control, submit via EcrecoverAuthorizer, assert midnight.isAuthorized(victim, attacker) == true.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged mempool observer, under the precondition that a victim has broadcast a valid signed Authorization transaction, front-run the victim's transaction by submitting the identical (auth, sig) calldata first, causing the victim's transaction to revert with InvalidNonce() while the authorization effect is already applied, violating the invariant that a user who signs and submits a valid authorization must be able to execute it themselves, causing scoped impact: victim's transaction reverts and they lose gas; if the victim signed a sequence of authorizations (nonce N, N+1, ...) the front-run of nonce N causes all subsequent pre-signed messages to remain valid but the victim's own submission of nonce N fails, creating confusion about which authorizations are pending? Proof idea: fork test where attacker front-runs victim's setIsAuthorized call with identical calldata, assert victim tx reverts with InvalidNonce, assert authorization was applied by attacker.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged Midnight-authorized operator, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][operator] == true and isAuthorized[victim][address(ecrecoverAuthorizer)] == true, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), isAuthorized=false} signed by operator, causing midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), false, victim) to execute, violating the invariant that a user who has set up EcrecoverAuthorizer as their sig-based auth path must be able to continue using it, causing scoped impact: operator can permanently freeze the victim's ability to use EcrecoverAuthorizer for future sig-based authorization changes, forcing them to submit an on-chain transaction to re-enable it? Proof idea: unit test where operator revokes EcrecoverAuthorizer's authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer itself, assert subsequent valid signed Authorization calls revert with Unauthorized from Midnight.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged Midnight-authorized operator, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][operator] == true and isAuthorized[victim][address(ecrecoverAuthorizer)] == true, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=false} signed by operator, causing midnight.setIsAuthorized(operator, false, victim) to execute and revoke the operator's own Midnight authorization, violating the invariant that an operator cannot use EcrecoverAuthorizer to self-revoke in a way that surprises the authorizer, causing scoped impact: operator can self-revoke their Midnight authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer, which may be unexpected behavior for integrations that assume only the authorizer can revoke? Proof idea: unit test where operator signs Authorization revoking themselves, submit via EcrecoverAuthorizer, assert midnight.isAuthorized(victim, operator) == false.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that EcrecoverAuthorizer is authorized in Midnight by victim and victim has signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=type(uint256).max}, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with a signature-malleable variant (flipped s value, adjusted v) of the victim's signature, causing the authorization to be applied via the malleable signature before the victim's original transaction, violating the invariant that each signed authorization message should be executable exactly once by the intended submitter, causing scoped impact: attacker front-runs with malleable signature, victim's original transaction fails with InvalidNonce, victim loses gas and may be confused about authorization state? Proof idea: compute high-s malleable variant of victim's ECDSA signature, submit via EcrecoverAuthorizer, assert it succeeds and victim's original tx fails.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged Midnight-authorized operator, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][operator] == true and isAuthorized[victim][address(ecrecoverAuthorizer)] == true, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=maliciousContract, isAuthorized=true} signed by operator, causing midnight.setIsAuthorized(maliciousContract, true, victim) to execute, violating the invariant that authorization grants via EcrecoverAuthorizer should only be possible with the authorizer's own key, causing scoped impact: maliciousContract gains full Midnight control over victim's positions, collateral, credit, debt, and consumed state, enabling withdrawal of all victim assets? Proof idea: unit test where operator grants maliciousContract authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer, assert maliciousContract can call midnight.withdrawCollateral on victim's behalf.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that authorization.authorizer == address(0) and a valid EIP-712 signature is crafted for address(0) (which ecrecover returns for invalid signatures), trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized where signer == address(0) is caught by require(signer != address(0), InvalidSignature()) but nonce[address(0)] has already been incremented at line 26, violating the invariant that nonce increments must only occur for valid signature paths, causing scoped impact: nonce[address(0)] is permanently incremented on every failed zero-signer attempt, which is harmless for address(0) but demonstrates the nonce-before-verify ordering risk? Proof idea: fuzz authorization.authorizer=address(0) with invalid signature bytes, assert nonce[address(0)] does not change after revert.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that EcrecoverAuthorizer is authorized in Midnight by victim and victim has signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=victim, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N}, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with this self-authorization, causing nonce[victim] to advance to N+1 while midnight.setIsAuthorized(victim, true, victim) executes as a no-op (isAuthorized[victim][victim] is set to true but victim is already msg.sender for all Midnight calls), violating the invariant that nonce consumption must correspond to a meaningful state change, causing scoped impact: victim's nonce is consumed for a no-op self-authorization, invalidating any other pre-signed authorization at nonce N that the victim intended to use? Proof idea: unit test where victim signs self-authorization at nonce 0, submit it, assert nonce advances to 1 and any other pre-signed message at nonce 0 is now invalid.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.isAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that victim has authorized operatorA in Midnight and operatorA has authorized operatorB in Midnight on behalf of victim (since authorized accounts can authorize other accounts per Midnight NatSpec), trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true} signed by operatorB (accepted via IMidnight(MIDNIGHT).isAuthorized(victim, operatorB) == true), causing midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim) to execute, violating the invariant that delegation chains through EcrecoverAuthorizer should not allow transitive authorization escalation, causing scoped impact: attacker gains full Midnight control over victim via a multi-hop delegation chain that victim never explicitly approved for EcrecoverAuthorizer use? Proof idea: unit test with victim->operatorA->operatorB delegation chain, operatorB signs EcrecoverAuthorizer message granting attacker control, assert midnight.isAuthorized(victim, attacker) == true.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that victim has signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=block.timestamp + 365 days} and later wants to cancel it by revoking EcrecoverAuthorizer's Midnight authorization, trigger a race condition where the attacker submits the victim's signed authorization in the same block as the victim's revocation transaction, causing the authorization to be applied after the victim believed they had cancelled it, violating the invariant that a user who revokes EcrecoverAuthorizer's Midnight authorization should be able to prevent any pending signed authorizations from executing, causing scoped impact: victim's revocation is ineffective if attacker front-runs with the victim's pre-signed authorization, granting X full Midnight control? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that victim has signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=type(uint256).max} and shared it off-chain, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized at any future time (even years later) as long as the deadline has not passed and EcrecoverAuthorizer remains authorized in Midnight, violating the invariant that a user who has not explicitly cancelled a signed authorization (by advancing their nonce) cannot prevent its eventual execution, causing scoped impact: victim who signed a long-deadline authorization and forgot about it loses Midnight control to X when the authorization is submitted long after signing? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that victim has authorized EcrecoverAuthorizer in Midnight and signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0}, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized followed immediately by midnight.setIsAuthorized(address(ecrecoverAuthorizer), false, victim) called by X (who now has full Midnight control), causing EcrecoverAuthorizer to be de-authorized in Midnight, violating the invariant that a user who grants authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer should retain the ability to revoke it via EcrecoverAuthorizer, causing scoped impact: X immediately revokes EcrecoverAuthorizer's Midnight authorization after gaining control, permanently preventing victim from using EcrecoverAuthorizer to revoke X's authorization, forcing victim to submit an on-chain transaction directly? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that EIP712_DOMAIN_TYPEHASH only commits to chainId and verifyingContract (no name or version fields), trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized on a different EcrecoverAuthorizer deployment (different address, same chainId) with a signature intended for the original deployment, violating the invariant that a signature for one EcrecoverAuthorizer deployment must not be replayable on another deployment, causing scoped impact: if a user signs an authorization for EcrecoverAuthorizer_v1 and a new EcrecoverAuthorizer_v2 is deployed at a different address, the signature is NOT replayable (different verifyingContract), but if two deployments share the same address (impossible) or if the user is confused about which deployment to sign for, they may sign for the wrong one? Proof idea: deploy two EcrecoverAuthorizer instances, sign for one, attempt replay on the other, assert it fails due to different domain separator.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that nonce[authorizer] is per-authorizer (not per (authorizer, authorized) pair), trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0} to consume nonce 0, making any other pre-signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=Y, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0} permanently invalid, violating the invariant that a user should be able to have multiple pending signed authorizations for different targets simultaneously, causing scoped impact: victim who signed multiple authorizations for different targets at the same nonce can only execute one; the others are permanently invalidated? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized / IMidnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged Midnight-authorized operator, under the precondition that isAuthorized[victim][operator] == true and isAuthorized[victim][address(ecrecoverAuthorizer)] == true, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=operator, isAuthorized=true} signed by operator (self-grant), causing midnight.setIsAuthorized(operator, true, victim) to execute as a no-op (already authorized), but consuming victim's nonce, violating the invariant that nonce consumption must correspond to a net state change, causing scoped impact: operator can grief victim by consuming nonces with no-op self-grant authorizations, invalidating victim's pre-signed authorization messages? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that victim has signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=false, nonce=0} to revoke X's authorization, and attacker has observed this signed revocation in the mempool, trigger a front-run where attacker first submits Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=Y, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0} (if attacker has a valid signature for nonce 0 from victim), consuming nonce 0 and causing victim's revocation to fail with InvalidNonce, violating the invariant that a user's signed revocation must be executable, causing scoped impact: victim's attempt to revoke X's authorization fails because attacker consumed nonce 0 with a different authorization, leaving X authorized? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that victim has signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0, deadline=T} and the current block.timestamp == T (deadline boundary), trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized where require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline) passes at exactly T, causing the authorization to execute at the deadline boundary, violating the invariant that a deadline of T should mean the authorization expires at T (exclusive), causing scoped impact: authorization executes at exactly the deadline timestamp, which may be later than the user intended if they expected deadline to be exclusive? [File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol -> Scope: Medium Periphery integration bug that can cause user loss when interacting with the core Midnight contract as intended'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that victim has signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=true, nonce=0} and separately signed Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=X, isAuthorized=false, nonce=1}, trigger EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized for nonce=0 (grant) and then for nonce=1 (revoke) in the same block via multicall or sequential transactions, causing the net effect to be isAuthorized[victim][X] == false, violating no invariant but demonstrating that the nonce ordering is critical for correct authorization state, causing scoped impact: if the order of submission is reversed (revoke at nonce=0, grant at nonce=1), the net effect is isAuthorized[victim][X] == true, which may not match victim's intent if they signed in a different order?
